**The dependency structure is a fibration (with optic edges) over the free category generated by the typed graph.**

The lived cascade after Q7 is the diagnostic: a state change did not merely mark reachable nodes. It triggered *typed, conditional, intermediate-sensitive* obligations—“must re-examine,” “review (may still hold),” “mechanical update”—and the process could short-circuit. Pure reachability on a static graph cannot express that. The tower already predicted a Level-1 superstructure; the phenomenon shows what must live there.

### 1. Evaluation of the four candidates

**(a) Transitive closure of a typed relation**  
This is the data structure we already have (`_reach.py` + frontmatter). Edge types form a small monoid (or category) of propagation actions under composition:

- Must ∘ anything = Must  
- Review ∘ Review = Review  
- Mechanical is absorbing in the forward direction, etc.

Reachability with type-composed labels gives a static “blast-radius set.” It is necessary and implementable today. It is not sufficient. Because a justification edge is *advisory*, the actual cascade is a dynamic process: after the intermediate node is reviewed, its state may be unchanged, extinguishing further propagation. Static transitive closure cannot see the intermediate outcome. Hence the deflationary answer is the underlying skeleton, not the superstructure.

**(b) Presheaf**  
A functor \(C^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathbf{Set}\) (or to a category of state spaces) that assigns to each item the set of things that currently depend on it, with restriction maps along dependency edges, captures the *static* “what varies over the base.” One can even impose a Grothendieck topology whose covering families are the obligatory-premise edges and ask for a sheaf condition (“a resolution is consistent across its dependents”).  

Useful for the global consistency question, but secondary for the dynamics. Propagation of a *change* is not restriction; it is transport of structure along a base morphism. Presheaves do not natively supply the lifts that turn a premise-state change into an obligation (or a possible non-change) in the dependent. They describe the “sets of dependents”; they do not explain the typed force of the cascade.

**(c) Fibration**  
This is the best primary model.  

Let \(B\) be the free category on the directed graph whose objects are the items (questions, claims, decisions) and whose generating morphisms are the typed edges. A cloven fibration \(p:E\to B\) has:

- fibres \(E_x =\) possible states of item \(x\) (including any “needs-review” flags one chooses to internalise);  
- for each generating edge \(e:x\to y\) of type \(\tau\), a chosen cartesian lift (or a family of lifts) that encodes the propagation semantics of \(\tau\).

- Premise edge \(\mapsto\) cartesian lift that *forces* re-examination (the identity lift on the old state is not cartesian).  
- Justification edge \(\mapsto\) a weaker or optional lift: the cleavage may offer both a “re-examine” cartesian arrow and an “still-holds” non-cartesian arrow; the process chooses after inspection.  
- Mechanical edge \(\mapsto\) a lift that is an actual state-update function, no human in the loop.

The fibration axioms guarantee that lifts compose correctly along paths, recovering a well-behaved notion of “propagation along a composite dependency.” The question “does this decision still hold after its premise moved?” is exactly the question whether the chosen lift of the base change lands in the subcategory of “valid” or “accepted” states in the fibre.  

Advisory edges do break *strict* functoriality of a naïve “state assignment” functor \(B\to\mathbf{Set}\). Two standard recoveries:

- Enrich every fibre with an obligation/flag component. Every edge then has a *strict* cartesian lift that at least sets the appropriate flag; the human (or later automated) clearing of the flag is a separate vertical morphism in the fibre. Functoriality and the fibration axioms are restored.  
- Work with a more general displayed category or a relational fibration in which lifts are relations rather than functions; the “may still hold” is then primitive.

Either route is clean. The first is probably the one the registry should implement.

**(d) Dependency edge as optic**  
Compatible and attractive for self-similarity.  

Q7 established that a transition is a Prism (an optic). A dependency edge likewise has a bidirectional character:

- forward: given a change (or a delta) in the premise, produce the obligatory update / review token / mechanical rewrite in the dependent;  
- backward: given the dependent, measure exposure (the “view” of all premises it rests on, or the cardinality of that view).

That is precisely the data of an optic (most often a lens or an affine traversal once flags and partiality are present; a Prism if the edge can “fail to match” in the advisory case).  

If every generating edge is an optic between the state spaces of its endpoints, then:

- path composition is optic composition,  
- the whole dependency structure is a diagram of optics in the category of state spaces,  
- blast-radius-before-building is the support of the composite optic (or the number of non-identity components).

The tower becomes self-similar: Level 0 transitions are optics; Level 1 dependencies are optics between those. This is not forced, but it is elegant and unifies the two layers under one gadget. It also sits comfortably inside the fibration picture: the cleavage of the fibration can be given by the forward parts of the optics.

### 2. Direct answers to the sharp questions

1. Best model: **(c) a fibration** (cloven, or a displayed category) whose base is the free category on the typed graph. The typed graph itself (a) is the generating data; the optics view (d) is a concrete description of the individual lifts; a presheaf (b) is the static shadow of the same structure.

2. Yes, the typing—especially the obligatory/advisory distinction—forces structure richer than a plain directed graph. Advisory edges make naïve composition of state-changes non-functorial. Recovery is either (i) internalise review obligations into the fibres so that every edge becomes a strict cartesian lift, or (ii) use relational/lax lifts. In both cases the fibration (or displayed category) axioms restore coherent composition of paths.

3. Yes. A dependency edge is usefully an optic (forward = propagate / set obligation, backward = audit exposure). This mirrors the Prism result of Q7 one level up and makes the growth tower self-similar. Whether one *defines* the fibration via a network of optics or merely notes the coincidence is a matter of taste; both descriptions are available.

### 3. What this buys the project

- **RUNG-CT** can now name Level ≥1: the dependency superstructure *is* the fibration of states (and obligations) over the free category of typed edges. The earlier “arrows in Cat / Fun” language is the correct ambient setting; the concrete fibrewise transport is what was missing.
- **RUNG-RS / registry**: the frontmatter edges should eventually carry the data the cleavage needs (not merely a type label, but the optic or the lift rule). `_reach.py` should compute *transport of obligations* (or the support of the composite optic), not merely boolean reachability. The present mechanism is the correct 0-approximation; the fibration tells us what to grow it into.
- **Blast-radius-as-a-number** receives a definition: the number of non-trivial cartesian lifts (or the size of the support of the optic) triggered by a hypothetical state change at a given node. It is no longer an ad-hoc count of files; it is the measure of the fibrewise change.

The registry remains the operative stopgap. The question was never “replace it tomorrow”; it was “what is the structure it is approximating, so that when we harden it we harden the right thing.” The answer is a fibration whose edges are optics. That is the layer that was missing.
